prompt: I'm building a parser that holds a String buffer and a &str slice pointing into it. I added a lifetime parameter to my struct, but the constructor fails to compile because the local variable `input` doesn't live long enough.
explanation: Self-referential structs — where a field holds a reference into another field of the same struct — cannot be expressed with Rust lifetimes. The lifetime 'a is external to the function; the local variable `input` lives only for the function body and can never satisfy 'a. Even if the lifetime bound were relaxed, moving `input` into the struct would move the allocation, invalidating the reference. The idiomatic solution is to store a byte offset (usize) instead of a direct slice. The current() method computes the &str on demand by re-borrowing self.input, which is safe because the borrow is tied to &self.

prompt: I want to return a struct that packages both an owned String and a &str slice into its first three bytes. The compiler rejects my function even after I add a lifetime annotation.
error_message:
error[E0106]: missing lifetime specifier
 --> src/main.rs:1:34
  |
1 | fn make_prefixed(data: String) -> (String, &str) {
  |                                            ^ expected named lifetime parameter
  |
help: consider introducing a named lifetime parameter
  |
1 | fn make_prefixed<'a>(data: String) -> (String, &'a str) {
explanation: Adding a lifetime annotation like `fn make_prefixed<'a>(data: String) -> (String, &'a str)` only moves the goal post: now you need an &'a str to satisfy 'a, but prefix borrows `data` which is local to the function, so it still cannot satisfy an external lifetime. Rust has no way to express "this &str borrows the String in the same tuple". The solution is to return owned data: clone the prefix into a new String. Alternatively, wrap both in a struct where a method computes the slice on demand.

prompt: I'm trying to build a Tokenizer that holds both a String and a Lines iterator over it. The iterator borrows the string, but when I try to move the string into the struct at the same time, the compiler complains.
error_message:
error[E0505]: cannot move out of `input` because it is borrowed
 --> src/main.rs:9:29
  |
8 |         let lines = input.lines();
  |                     ----- borrow of `input` occurs here
9 |         Tokenizer { source: input, lines }
  |                             ^^^^^ move out of `input` occurs here
  |                                   borrow later used here
explanation: Borrowing `input` to create a `Lines` iterator and then moving `input` into the struct at the same time violates the borrow rules: the move ends the data's life at the old address, which would dangle the iterator. This is the core impossibility of self-referential structs in safe Rust. The fix is to store the position (line_pos: usize) and recompute the slice on every call to next_line(), which borrows from self.source — a stable location — rather than from a transient local.

prompt: I'm building a tree where each node holds a reference to its parent. I annotated the struct with a lifetime and everything seems right, but when I try to return a child node from a function I get a lifetime error.
explanation: A `Node<'static>` would require its parent reference to point to memory with 'static lifetime. Local variables never satisfy 'static. More fundamentally, parent-child pointer cycles cannot be expressed with Rust lifetimes at all — the parent would need to outlive the child but also reference the child, creating a paradox. The standard solution is `Rc<RefCell<Node>>` for shared ownership with interior mutability. For trees with back-edges to parents, consider Rc for the downward direction and Weak for the upward direction to avoid reference cycles that prevent deallocation.

prompt: I used Pin<Box<T>> with PhantomPinned to create a self-referential struct. The initialization works, but when I try to move the value out of the Pin to consume it, I get an error about moving out of a Pin.
error_message:
error[E0507]: cannot move out of `*pinned` which is behind a `Pin<Box<SelfRef>>`
 --> src/main.rs:23:26
  |
23|     let inner: SelfRef = *pinned;
  |                          ^^^^^^^ move occurs because `*pinned` has type `SelfRef`,
  |                          which does not implement the `Copy` trait
explanation: Pin<Box<T>> guarantees that the heap allocation will never move for the lifetime of the Pin. Moving out of `*pinned` would violate that guarantee — `ptr` would immediately dangle after the move. Rust enforces this by preventing DerefMut access that would allow taking ownership when T: !Unpin (which PhantomPinned imposes). The correct pattern is to access self-referential structs only through Pin<&Self> or Pin<&mut Self> references, never by moving out. Methods should take `self: Pin<&Self>` and use get_ref() to borrow fields.

prompt: I want a struct that holds an owned String and a &str pointing into it for zero-copy access after parsing.
error_message: missing lifetime specifier
explanation: Rust has no lifetime that means 'borrows from another field of the same struct'. Storing byte offsets and recomputing the slice in a method ties the output lifetime to &self, which is safe and moves freely.

prompt: I'm trying to return a struct that contains both an owned String and a &str pointing into that String, but the compiler won't allow it.
error_message: cannot borrow `v.data` because it is also borrowed as part of `v`
explanation: Lifetimes are per-value, not per-field, so you can never express 'this reference borrows from a sibling field'. The Pin<Box<T>> pattern keeps the allocation address stable so a raw pointer into data remains valid. PhantomPinned prevents the type from implementing Unpin and blocks accidental moves.

prompt: I'm building a doubly-linked list and want each node to hold a &mut reference to its previous and next sibling, but I get borrow errors everywhere.
error_message: cannot borrow `a` as mutable more than once at a time
explanation: Aliasing &mut references are unconditionally forbidden in safe Rust. A doubly-linked list needs two mutable paths to the same node (its predecessor and successor both point to it), which violates this rule. The idiomatic safe solution stores Vec indices; the Vec owns all nodes and the indices are plain Copy integers.

prompt: I want a tree where each node holds a reference to its parent so I can walk upward, but I can't figure out the lifetimes.
error_message: value does not live long enough; `root` dropped while still borrowed
explanation: A node that is both owned by its parent and holds a reference to its parent creates a cycle that Rust's lifetime rules cannot express. An arena (Vec of nodes) with integer indices breaks the cycle: the Vec owns all nodes, and parent/child relationships are index values that carry no borrow.

prompt: I'm implementing a graph and want each Node to hold Vec<&Node> for its neighbors, but adding edges causes lifetime and borrow errors.
error_message: cannot borrow `a` as immutable because it is also borrowed as mutable
explanation: Cycles in reference graphs break Rust's DAG-shaped borrow checker. Representing edges as (usize, usize) pairs or as adjacency lists of node IDs completely sidesteps borrow issues. All nodes live in the graph struct; edges are plain integers.

prompt: I have a struct that owns a Vec<u8> buffer and I want to hold a &[u8] window into it as a field for fast repeated access.
error_message: missing lifetime specifier
explanation: A slice field would borrow from the Vec field at the same time as the struct owns it. Using start/end indices is equivalent in performance (single add and a range check) and lets the struct move freely.

prompt: I want a struct that caches a parsed view of its own string field. Adding a lifetime parameter creates a recursive mess.
error_message: cannot move out of `source` because it is borrowed
explanation: You cannot move a value into a struct field while simultaneously borrowing that same value into another field. Storing (start, end) byte pairs for each line is allocation-free, the struct moves freely, and line() derives a valid borrow from &self at call time.

prompt: I need a pinned self-referential struct but forgot to add PhantomPinned, causing it to accidentally implement Unpin and allowing moves that corrupt the pointer.
error_message: Pin::into_inner succeeds because SelfRef implements Unpin (auto-trait), allowing a move that leaves ptr dangling — undefined behaviour at runtime.
explanation: PhantomPinned is a zero-sized marker that opts the type out of the Unpin auto-trait. Without it, Pin<Box<SelfRef>> is just a thin wrapper you can unwrap with Pin::into_inner, moving the allocation and leaving any stored raw pointers dangling.

prompt: I'm trying to use the ouroboros crate to safely build a self-referential struct but the macro usage is wrong.
error_message: expected named lifetime parameter; the `#[borrows]` field must use the `'this` lifetime
explanation: ouroboros uses the special 'this lifetime to express that a field borrows from a sibling field in the same struct. Every borrowed field must be annotated with &'this T (or another 'this-bound type) and tagged with #[covariant] or #[not_covariant] so the macro can generate safe accessor closures.

prompt: I want to use the self_cell crate to build a struct with an owner and a dependent borrowed from it, but the initialization looks wrong.
error_message: missing lifetime specifier on &str inside the dependent type
explanation: self_cell introduces the 'owner lifetime to refer to the owner field. The dependent type must use 'owner for any references that borrow from the owner. Without it the compiler sees an unannotated &str and emits E0106.

prompt: I'm building an async state machine where a Future holds a reference into a local buffer it also owns. Pin seems necessary but I'm not sure how to wire it up.
error_message: self-referential borrows across await points require the future to be pinned; moving an unpinned future invalidates internal references
explanation: Async state machines compiled by rustc can contain self-referential borrows across await points. The compiler requires the Future to be pinned before polling; Box::pin + PhantomPinned together prevent accidental moves that would dangle those internal pointers.

prompt: I want to pin a value on the stack using the pin! macro and then call a method that requires Pin<&mut T>, but I get errors about the variable not being pinned.
error_message: `NeedsPin` cannot be unpinned; `Pin::new` requires `T: Unpin`
explanation: Pin::new requires T: Unpin. For !Unpin types you must use the pin! macro (stable since 1.68) or Box::pin. pin! shadow-rebinds the local variable to a Pin<&mut T> without a heap allocation, so the value stays on the stack at a stable address for the rest of the scope.

prompt: I'm implementing pin_project to expose pinned projections of struct fields without writing unsafe code manually, but the derive is rejected.
error_message: could not find `pin_project` in the list of imports
explanation: pin_project is an external crate that generates safe projections from Pin<&mut Self> to Pin<&mut Field> for #[pin] fields and &mut Field for unpinned fields. Without it in Cargo.toml the macro is unavailable. project() lets you access pinned fields without any unsafe blocks in your impl.

prompt: I'm trying to build a slab allocator where each slot can hold a reference to another slot, creating a self-referential pool, but Rust rejects the lifetime annotations.
error_message: lifetime may not live long enough; `'a` must outlive the borrow introduced by pushing into `slots`
explanation: Self-referential slot chains are impossible with safe Rust references. A classic slab uses a flat Vec for data and a VecDeque of free indices; links between slots are encoded as integers, not pointers.

prompt: I want to build an iterator that holds a &mut reference to the collection it's iterating over, stored as a field on the same struct that owns the collection.
error_message: missing lifetime specifier; cannot borrow `data` as immutable after it has been moved
explanation: An iterator cannot hold a reference into the collection it also owns; the reference would borrow the already-moved field. Instead, store a position cursor and advance it in next(). The lifetime of any yielded item then comes from &mut self, which is correct.

prompt: I need to use typed-arena for arena allocation so references into the arena live as long as the arena itself, and I want a struct that holds both the arena and the refs it produces.
error_message: cannot borrow `w.arena` as mutable because it is also borrowed as part of `w.names`
explanation: Storing the arena and the references it produces in the same struct is a self-referential pattern. The fix is to keep the arena as a separate local binding. Because the arena is declared before w, it outlives it, so the 'a lifetime on World<'a> correctly ties the references to the arena's scope.

prompt: I want a struct that wraps Rc<RefCell<Self>> to build a node graph with shared mutable ownership, but the recursive type definition doesn't compile.
error_message: recursive type `Node` has infinite size
explanation: A recursive struct must break the infinite-size chain with heap indirection. Vec<Rc<RefCell<Node>>> satisfies the size requirement (Vec is a pointer + length) while providing shared ownership (Rc) and interior mutability (RefCell) needed for a graph with multiple owners of each node.

prompt: I'm trying to store a reference to a sibling field inside a closure that's also a field of the same struct.
error_message: cannot move out of `data` because it is borrowed
explanation: A closure stored as a field cannot borrow another field of the same struct; the struct doesn't exist yet when the closure captures. Replacing the stored closure with a method call eliminates the self-reference entirely: the method borrows &self at call time, which is always valid.

prompt: I'm building a linked-list cursor that holds a &mut Node pointing somewhere inside the list, but the mutable borrow conflicts with the owned head field.
error_message: cannot borrow `c.head` as mutable because it is also borrowed as part of `c`
explanation: Storing a mutable reference into an owned sub-structure alongside the owner is self-referential and always rejected. Instead, return the reference from a method: cursor() borrows &mut self for its duration, giving a &mut Node whose lifetime is bounded by the call, not stored in the struct.

prompt: I want to build a string interner that stores Arc<str> values and returns &str references that live as long as the interner itself.
error_message: mismatched types: expected `&str`, found `&Arc<str>`
explanation: Returning a &str tied to the interner's pool requires expressing a self-lifetime, which isn't possible in safe Rust. Returning Arc<str> is the idiomatic solution: callers get shared ownership without a borrow, and ptr_eq confirms deduplication.

prompt: I want to use slotmap so nodes in my graph can reference each other by stable keys without lifetime issues.
error_message: raw pointers into local variables are invalidated when those variables are moved into the Vec — undefined behaviour
explanation: Raw pointers are invalidated when the object they point to is moved. slotmap provides stable DefaultKey handles that remain valid as long as the entry hasn't been removed. Nodes reference each other by key, which is a copyable integer — no pointers, no lifetimes, no unsafe.

prompt: I want a self-referential struct where one field is a HashMap and another is a &mut reference to an entry in that HashMap for fast repeated updates.
error_message: missing lifetime specifier on `hot` field; and: cannot move `map` into struct because it is already borrowed
explanation: You can't store a &mut reference into a HashMap entry alongside the HashMap — the borrow checker forbids it. Store the key and re-derive the reference inside methods; the HashMap::entry API makes this fast and safe.

prompt: I'm trying to call Pin::get_unchecked_mut on a struct that still implements Unpin, which defeats the safety invariant.
error_message: No compiler error — but for a truly self-referential struct this pattern is unsound; Pin provides no address-stability guarantee for Unpin types because Pin::into_inner is always available.
explanation: PhantomPinned opts the type out of Unpin, making Pin::into_inner a compile error and ensuring that once the value is pinned, it cannot be moved. For Unpin types, pinning is transparent and provides no address-stability; only !Unpin types actually need the pin contract.

prompt: I want to store a reference to a regex match inside the same struct that owns the input string, so I can return both together.
error_message: cannot move out of `input` because it is borrowed
explanation: You cannot borrow a value into a struct field and move that value into another field of the same struct in the same expression. Storing the match span as (start, end) indices and recomputing &str via a method avoids any self-referential borrow.

prompt: How do I safely construct a self-referential struct using raw pointers and Pin when I need to initialise the pointer field after the struct is placed in memory?
error_message: the address of `r.data` captured into `r.head` is invalidated when `r` is returned by value and moved to the call site
explanation: Two-step initialisation is mandatory for self-referential structs: first pin the allocation (which fixes the address), then store the pointer. Any attempt to set a self-pointer before pinning, or to return by value after setting it, produces a dangling pointer.
